This interactive workshop is designed to help professionals across all roles—whether in strategy, design, content creation, or management—embed accessibility into their digital products. Participants will gain a deep understanding of accessibility principles and learn how to apply them to various digital environments, including websites, mobile apps, and digital marketing materials. The workshop will cover practical strategies and tools to integrate accessibility at every stage of the digital product process, ensuring an inclusive experience for all users.

This workshop aims to empower participants to become advocates for accessibility in their digital projects, regardless of their technical expertise or role, by providing them with the knowledge, tools, and confidence to make digital products more inclusive.

Summary: Digital access is a civil right of disabled people. This session shares U.S. legal updates about access to websites, apps, kiosks, and more, and about laws, regulations, lawsuits, negotiations, and government policies.

Detailed Description: There is a lot happening in the digital accessibility legal space in the United States. Designed for all levels of experience, this session will offer a lightning overview of recent legal developments in the US impacting access to websites, mobile apps, kiosks, healthcare, and more. We’ll look at federal government regulations, lawsuits, Structured Negotiations, state laws, and federal agency guidance and peek into the future to see what to expect in the coming year.

Digital accessibility is a civil right of disabled people, an ethical imperative, a global legal mandate, and good for business. Come learn how the law can help you advocate for an inclusive digital world.
Lainey Feingold, Law Office of Lainey Feingold

Whether you're a long-time attendee or joining us for the first time, the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference stands as the pinnacle gathering in its field. Celebrating four decades of excellence, this conference is renowned as the premier forum for exploring the latest in technology and accessibility for persons with disabilities.

Since its inception, California State University, Northridge's Center on Disabilities has proudly hosted this event, drawing researchers, educators, practitioners, and exhibitors from across the globe. Together, we foster an environment where knowledge, innovation, and best practices converge to advance the cause of inclusion.

Join us in Anaheim, California for an unforgettable week packed with Pre-Conference Workshops, a dynamic Keynote Address Program, engaging Featured Presentations, enriching General Sessions, and vibrant Sponsor Showcase Suites. The CSUN AT Conference sessions have been approved for 2.0 to 2.8 Continuing Education Units (CEU) by San Diego State University (SDSU). The primary purpose of the CEU is to provide a permanent record of the educational accomplishments of an individual who has completed a non-credit educational program. This record can be used to give employers, professional associations and societies a uniform measure of short-term educational programs completed by an individual. CEUs are not academic units and do not appear on an SDSU transcript, and cannot be applied to degree work at institutions of higher education.

CEUs are $50 per 1.0 CEU, paid directly to SDSU (1.0 contact hour is equivalent to 10 hours of participation). The CEUs offered are general CEUs and it is the responsibility of the attendee to determine whether the CEUs can be applied toward any professional organization.

CEUs are offered only in the following increments based on participation. You are eligible to receive credits for conference and pre-conference workshop participation. No other increments are available.

There is a maximum of 20 hours of participation available through attendance at the conference sessions. If you attend a pre-conference workshop, a half-day workshop earns a total of 4 hours and a full-day workshop earns a total of 8 hours. Please consider your anticipated participation when determining the appropriate increment to purchase.

Mike has worked in the fields of accessibility, assistive technology, and disability services for over 15 years. Mike has a Master of Science degree in Assistive Technology and Human Services from California State University Northridge. At the University of Colorado, Mike oversees the digital accessibility assessment, usability, training, and outreach activities within the Digital Accessibility Office, his portfolio includes supervision of accessibility testing, procurement, training, and consulting efforts. Mike also teaches multiple courses in accessibility and assistive technology at California State University, Northridge.

This workshop will explore the role of Assistive Technology (AT) in fostering an inclusive learning environment. We’ll provide an overview of AT and how it not only supports students with disabilities but enhances the educational experience for all students. Understanding AT only takes us so far. Many times, these barriers are created unknowingly and unintentionally. So, we will also provide an overview of accessibility barriers experienced by AT users when interacting with inaccessible content. We will explore design choices and assess if they will hinder or promote accessibility.

In this interactive workshop, we will explore the principles of accessible content creation using the POUR framework (Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust). Attendees will leave equipped with practical strategies and best practices, including the use of meaningful hyperlinks, alternative text for images, structured tables, and accessible styles - headings, font selection, font size, color, etc. We will cover accessibility in various formats, including Word, PowerPoint, and PDFs. By embracing these best practices, attendees will be equipped to ensure an accessible learning experience for all students, especially students with disabilities.

The CSUN AT Conference sessions have been approved for 2.0 to 2.8 Continuing Education Units (CEU) by San Diego State University (SDSU). The primary purpose of the CEU is to provide a permanent record of the educational accomplishments of an individual who has completed a non-credit educational program. This record can be used to give employers, professional associations and societies a uniform measure of short-term educational programs completed by an individual. CEUs are not academic units and do not appear on an SDSU transcript, and cannot be applied to degree work at institutions of higher education.

CEUs are $50 per 1.0 CEU, paid directly to SDSU (1.0 contact hour is equivalent to 10 hours of participation). The CEUs offered are general CEUs and it is the responsibility of the attendee to determine whether the CEUs can be applied toward any professional organization.

CEUs are offered only in the following increments based on participation. You are eligible to receive credits for conference and pre-conference workshop participation. No other increments are available.

There is a maximum of 20 hours of participation available through attendance at the conference sessions. If you attend a pre-conference workshop, a half-day workshop earns a total of 4 hours and a full-day workshop earns a total of 8 hours. Please consider your anticipated participation when determining the appropriate increment to purchase.

The implementation of Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) systems is vital for preventing communication failures for individuals with profound autism. Speech-generating devices (SGDs) are frequently recommended to support communication and language development for non-speaking or minimally vocal autistic individuals. However, despite their potential, SGDs often experience high abandonment rates due to various challenges such as lack of AAC personalization, limited caregiver training, and difficulty in operational use. In this workshop, presenters will explore these barriers through a collaborative lens and offer actionable strategies to overcome them, ensuring more successful communication outcomes. A growing body of research supports the advancement of AAC technologies for individuals with autism and developmental disabilities.

Brittany is the Founder and CEO of Evergreen Communication Therapy and leads a dedicated team of professionals who are passionate advocates for individuals affected by aphasia. Summary: Although governed by different laws and policies, U.S. Federal and State Governments share accessibility goals. Learn how the U.S. Federal Government and the Washington State Government collaborate to advance digital accessibility from coast to coast.

Detailed Description: Washington, D.C., the home of the US Federal Government, and the State of Washington share more than a name. Both strive to implement accessible Information and Communication Technology. Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act requires a Federal agency to build, buy, and implement the most accessible ICT that meets its needs. It includes Technical Standards which define digital accessibility. While a State is not mandated to comply with Section 508 Technical Standards, it is encouraged to include relevant standards in its own ICT accessibility legislation or policies.

Presenters Mary Craig, CIO of Washington State Department of Services for the Blind, and Betsy Sirk, Digital Accessibility Director of NASA, first met at CSUN AT Conference 2022. Inspired by Ms. Sirk’s presentation on accessible ICT acquisitions, Ms. Craig became a champion for digital accessibility initiatives in Washington State. For example, she worked with the State CIO to build an Accessibility Community of Practice that allows collaboration and resource sharing. She also implemented an annual “Accessibility Matters” conference, which has become Washington’s premier accessibility event. Washington is also focused on updating its accessibility policy. Moving to the Federal Government, this presentation shares how a collaboration with a State helps government-wide outreach efforts to promote digital accessibility. Specific Industry Outreach efforts that benefit both Washingtons are highlighted.
Betsy Sirk, National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
Mary Craig, Washington State Department of Services for the Blind

In 2013, after the tragic mass shooting at Sandy Hook Elementary in Newtown, Connecticut, Gabby co-founded the organization today known as Giffords. Over the past decade, the organization has been a leader in the national gun safety movement, making gun safety a kitchen table issue for voters. Giffords has helped gun safety champions win up and down the ballot in local, state, and federal races and worked hard to pass lifesaving legislation in states across the country and in the US House of Representatives.

As she leads Giffords, Gabby continues to make remarkable progress in her recovery. She helps raise awareness about aphasia, the language impairment she has suffered from since her shooting. She enjoys spending her free time playing the French horn, biking, practicing yoga, learning Spanish, and spending time with her husband, Senator Mark Kelly, her stepdaughters, Claudia and Claire, and being a “GG” to Claudia’s daughter, Sage.

Former Congresswoman Gabrielle Giffords understands what it’s like to face overwhelming barriers. She also understands what it takes to get past them. From her scrappy Arizona childhood, to her bridge-building politics, to her remarkable recovery from traumatic injuries, Gabby personifies courage. Today she leads Giffords, the gun violence prevention organization she co-founded. Giffords is a nonprofit organization that shifts culture, changes policies, and challenges injustice, inspiring Americans across the country to fight gun violence.

The Center on Disabilities annually recognizes a leader who has made a significant impact in education and research. This year, we are pleased to honor David L. Jaffe.

David L. Jaffe is an Adjunct Lecturer in the Mechanical Engineering Department at Stanford University where he has taught Perspectives in Assistive Technology for the past 18 years. The course explores the broad spectrum of issues surrounding the design, development, and use of technology that benefits people with disabilities and older adults. Students pursue projects that address a challenge experienced by these individuals who live in the local community by designing and fabricating a functional assistive technology device. Guest lecturers, a field trip, an Assistive Technology Fair, a film screening, student project presentations, and an end-of-term project demonstration are the basis of the class sessions.

Please be aware of the following:
All Registrations are non-transferable and substitutions are not permitted.
Exhibitor Complimentary Registrations are NOT VALID for Presenter Registration fees.
Credit card is the only accepted form of payment.
There are several discounts available for presenters. All discounts must be requested prior to registering. Visit the Presenter Discounts Page for eligibility information and guidelines.
Join us for a fireside chat about the future of AI and accessibility.
Brandon Johnson, Davis Wright Tremaine LLP
The CSUNATC.TV is now available. Check out our Keynote Address Program and Featured Presentations.
